retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Pathophysiology -- Cold Agglutinin Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Cold agglutinin disease is a rare autoimmune disorder characterized by autoantibodies, primarily of the IgM class, which exhibit reactivity against RBCs at temperatures below normal body temperature. Occasionally, IgG and IgA cold agglutinins have been reported, although IgA cold agglutinin does not cause cold agglutinin disease. The pathophysiology involves a complex interplay of immune mechanisms that culminate in the destruction of RBCs and subsequent anemia. [11] [18] [19] The underlying trigger for cold agglutinin disease is the production of autoantibodies by the immune system. [20] These autoantibodies typically target antigens, such as the I/i, GLOB, or P antigens, on the surface of RBCs.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Immune hemolytic anemias are categorized into autoimmune, alloimmune, and drug-induced mechanisms, each with unique etiologies and pathophysiological processes. Autoimmune hemolytic anemias (AIHAs) include warm AIHA, cold agglutinin disease, and mixed-type AIHA. The most common form of AIHA is warm AIHA, which is mediated by immunoglobulin G (IgG) antibodies and is often associated with hematologic, autoimmune, and infectious conditions. Cold agglutinin disease involves IgM antibodies that react at cold temperatures, and paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria features an IgG autoantibody causing hemolysis upon rewarming. Alloimmune hemolytic anemia occurs when alloantibodies attack foreign RBC antigens, typically following blood transfusions or during hemolytic disease of the newborn. Drug-induced immune hemolytic anemia (DIIHA) occurs when drug-induced antibodies bind to and destroy RBCs.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Pathophysiology -- Cold Agglutinin Disease
retrieved_docs.content: When the body is exposed to colder temperatures, particularly below 37 °C, these IgM autoantibodies bind to multiple RBCs, causing agglutination or clumping—a process known as cold agglutination. [21] This phenomenon is the hallmark of cold agglutinin disease and is responsible for many clinical manifestations. In cold agglutinin disease, IgM autoantibodies to RBCs bind to C1q, triggering activation of the complement cascade, a critical part of the innate immune response. [22] [23] Complement proteins, particularly C3b, are deposited on the surface of RBCs, which promotes their destruction. This complement activation enhances the opsonization of RBCs, making them more susceptible to phagocytosis by macrophages in the reticuloendothelial system, particularly in the liver by Kupffer cells, resulting in extravascular hemolysis. [24] Less commonly, in about 15% of cases, complement activation facilitates the formation of the membrane attack complex, leading to direct lysis of RBCs and subsequent hemoglobinuria, indicating intravascular hemolysis. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Etiology and Epidemiology -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia: AIHA occurs when the immune system produces autoantibodies that target self-antigens on RBCs, leading to their destruction. AIHA can be classified into warm AIHA, cold agglutinin disease, and mixed-type AIHA. Warm AIHA is the most common subtype, characterized by autoantibodies of the IgG class that react optimally at body temperature. However, IgA and warm-acting IgM have also been reported. Cold agglutinin disease involves cold-reactive IgM antibodies that agglutinate RBCs at low temperatures, causing intravascular hemolysis upon rewarming. Mixed-type AIHA exhibits features of both warm and cold antibody-mediated hemolysis.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Immune hemolytic anemias are disorders characterized by the immune system targeting and destroying red blood cells (RBCs). The etiology of these anemias is multifaceted, involving autoimmune, alloimmune, and drug-induced mechanisms. Furthermore, this leads to conditions such as warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA), cold agglutinin disease, mixed AIHA, paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria, and drug-induced hemolytic anemia. Diagnosing different types of immune hemolytic anemia requires a detailed interpretation of several laboratory tests, including complete blood count (CBC), haptoglobin, lactate dehydrogenase, reticulocyte count, unconjugated bilirubin, direct and indirect antiglobulin tests, and peripheral blood smears. Accurate diagnosis and management of these conditions through comprehensive laboratory evaluation improve patient outcomes and elevate the standard of care in hematology and transfusion medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Pathophysiology -- Cold Agglutinin Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Cold agglutinin disease is classified into primary (idiopathic) or secondary forms. Primary cold agglutinin disease involves cold agglutinin-mediated destruction of RBCs and extravascular hemolysis without an underlying disorder. Patients with primary cold agglutinin disease often have a low-grade clonal lymphoproliferative bone marrow disorder. [26] Secondary cold agglutinin syndrome is typically precipitated by an underlying infection, autoimmune disease, or overt lymphoma. In older patients, cold agglutinin disease often presents as a primary disease with an underlying lymphoid malignancy such as B-cell or plasma cell disorders, aggressive non-Hodgkin lymphoma, or Waldenström macroglobulinemia. Although solid tumors are rarely associated with primary cold agglutinin disease, this association is mostly uncommon. [27] Younger patients are more likely to have secondary cold agglutinin disease precipitated from infection, commonly M pneumoniae or Epstein-Barr infection. [28] [29] Cases have been reported of infections precipitating secondary cold agglutinin disease from HIV, influenza, rubella, varicella-zoster virus, and COVID-19. [30] [31] [32] [33] [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Coombs Test -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA): AIHA is traditionally the most recognized cause of positive antiglobulin testing, and has been the topic of extensive study. The classification “AIHA” serves as an overarching descriptor that unifies a large group of diagnoses with differing etiologies that cause hemolysis using antibodies against RBCs. [21] The classification can be dichotomized further by considering factors such as warm versus cold agglutination and primary versus secondary cause. AIHA may also be drug-induced or syndromic (see “Evans syndrome”). Further characterization of diseases that fall under this classification is beyond the scope of this entry.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosing the specific type of hemolytic anemia is crucial for tailoring effective management strategies and optimizing patient outcomes. Distinguishing between different etiologies, such as AIHA, alloimmune hemolytic anemia, and other forms of hemolysis, guides treatment decisions and interventions. Identifying whether the condition involves warm or cold antibody-mediated mechanisms in AIHA influences treatment selection. Warm AIHA, characterized by IgG-mediated hemolysis, typically responds well to initial treatment with glucocorticoids and rituximab, particularly in symptomatic patients. [3] Conversely, cold AIHA, involving IgM antibodies and complement activation, may necessitate different therapeutic approaches, such as avoidance of cold exposure and, in severe cases, plasmapheresis or immunosuppressive therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Pathophysiology -- Paroxysmal Cold Hemoglobinuria
retrieved_docs.content: Paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria is an acquired hemolytic anemia characterized by an IgG autoantibody that activates complement in cold temperatures, resulting in intravascular hemolysis upon rewarming, accompanied by hemoglobinuria. The exact mechanisms triggering autoantibody formation remain unclear, but this typically arises in the context of infections or autoimmune disorders, suggesting immune stimulation of autoantibody production. A proposed mechanism involves the generation of cross-reacting antibodies that target viral or bacterial antigens mimicking the P antigen on RBCs.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Interfering Factors -- Autoantibodies
retrieved_docs.content: Autoantibodies, such as cold agglutinins or warm-reactive autoantibodies, can complicate the interpretation of serological tests for immune hemolytic anemia. These autoantibodies may bind to RBCs nonspecifically, leading to false-positive results or masking the detection of specific alloantibodies. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Immunoglobulin M -- Clinical Significance -- Selective IgM Deficiency
retrieved_docs.content: Cold agglutinin disease (CAD) is a form of autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AHA) mediated predominantly by IgM antibodies. As the name of the disease implies, IgM recognizing these erythrocyte antigens can agglutinate erythrocytes at colder temperatures. IgG or IgM antibodies that bind to erythrocytes can lead to red blood cell destruction by either promoting opsonization and clearance of erythrocytes by phagocytes in the spleen or liver or by activating complement leading to erythrocyte lysis as a result of MAC formation. Autoantibodies can cause AHA, can be induced by an infection, or can be drug-induced, in which case antibodies recognize complexes of drugs bound to erythrocytes. CAD is a rare disease affecting approximately 16 per million and is predominantly associated with IgM autoantibodies directed against the blood group antigens large I and small i. [13] [14] These ubiquitously expressed antigens are polysaccharides, and due to their relatively weak non-covalent interactions with IgM, IgM binding to the antigens is significantly strengthed at lower temperatures when erythrocytes circulate in the periphery.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Pathophysiology -- Warm Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia
retrieved_docs.content: Warm AIHA occurs when the body's immune system mistakenly attacks its own RBCs, destroying them. This type of anemia can occur spontaneously without any apparent cause, known as primary or idiopathic warm AIHA, or it can develop secondary to certain underlying conditions or medications that trigger the production of autoantibodies targeting RBCs. The autoantibodies involved in warm AIHA are predominantly of the IgG type, although IgA and warm-acting IgM antibodies have also been reported. Subtypes of IgG, such as IgG1 and IgG3, can activate the complement system and cause more severe hemolysis than other subtypes. These antibodies typically target common antigens found on the surface of RBCs, with the most frequently targeted antigens including those of the Rh complex and glycophorin antigens, which are heavily glycosylated proteins on the membrane. [2] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Pathophysiology -- Paroxysmal Cold Hemoglobinuria
retrieved_docs.content: The autoantibody in paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria exhibits distinct characteristics, including specificity for the RBC GLOB antigen (formerly known as the P antigen)—a polysaccharide antigen on the RBC surface. [35] These antibodies are polyclonal, derived from multiple B-cell clones, and predominantly of the IgG class. [36] Unlike cold agglutinins, which are typically IgM antibodies that induce RBC agglutination, these antibodies do not cause agglutination but instead bind to RBCs below normal body temperature. Upon rewarming, the antibodies dissociate, but complement proteins remain attached to RBCs, leading to complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis—a hallmark of the anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Secondary Thrombocytosis -- Etiology -- Hemolysis
retrieved_docs.content: Most diseases with antibody-mediated hemolysis manifest thrombocytopenia. Conversely, some conditions, including cold agglutinin disease (CAD), cause thrombocytosis. Although classically associated with Mycoplasma pneumoniae , CAD is also linked to Epstein-Barr virus, cytomegalovirus, COVID-19, and malignant or lymphoproliferative diseases. [37] [38] Mycoplasma -associated hemolysis is due to cold agglutinins, the antibodies against the I antigen found on erythrocytes and respiratory endothelial cells. [39] [37] The IgM cold agglutinins bind to complement factor C1 and initiate the classic complement pathway. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Etiology and Epidemiology -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The epidemiology of AIHA varies depending on the underlying etiology. Warm AIHA is the most prevalent subtype, with a bimodal age distribution peaking in young adulthood and later in life. [9] Warm AIHA shows a slight predominance in women. [3] Cold agglutinin disease predominantly affects older adults in their late 60s and early 70s and is often secondary to underlying conditions such as infections or lymphoproliferative disorders. Cold agglutinin disease is slightly more prevalent in women and accounts for 15% to 30% of all hemolytic anemia cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Pathophysiology -- Mixed Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia
retrieved_docs.content: Mixed AIHA is a multifaceted hematological disorder characterized by the simultaneous presence of autoantibodies targeting different antigens on RBCs, resulting in hemolysis and anemia. [23] The immune pathology underlying mixed AIHA involves diverse mechanisms contributing to RBC destruction and associated clinical manifestations. Autoantibodies in mixed AIHA can target various RBC antigens, including Rh, Kell, Duffy, and others, reflecting the heterogeneous nature of the immune response in this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 955
retrieved_docs.content: Arthritis, acquired hemolytic anemia, cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia, coronary artery inflammation, and a form of hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity are all classified within the “Immune disorders/Autoimmune (SMQ)” grouping. This categorization reflects that these conditions are relevant to immune‑mediated disease processes and are monitored together in pharmacovigilance and clinical practice. While most of the listed disorders are driven by autoimmune mechanisms, the non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia is included in the same SMQ to ensure comprehensive safety surveillance of all hemolytic events that may arise in the context of immune system modulation. Recognizing the shared immunological context of these entities aids clinicians in identifying potential autoimmune contributions, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing therapeutic decisions across a spectrum of inflammatory and hematologic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2241
retrieved_docs.content: Autoimmune disorders classified under اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ) encompass a diverse range of conditions that share an underlying immune‑mediated pathophysiology. فقر الدم الوبيل (warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia) is included in this group, reflecting the immune system’s attack on red blood cells leading to hemolysis. فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold agglutinin disease) is also a member, representing a similar hemolytic process that is triggered by cold temperatures. التهاب المفاصل (arthritis) falls within the same SMQ category, illustrating how autoimmune mechanisms can target synovial joints and cause chronic inflammation and pain. Finally, التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation) is part of اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ), highlighting that autoimmune activity can affect vascular structures and contribute to coronary artery disease. Recognizing these entities—warm and cold autoimmune hemolytic anemias, arthritis, and coronary artery inflammation—as members of a common autoimmune disorder group underscores their shared immunological basis and supports integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 1252
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي), cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد), arthritis (التهاب المفاصل), and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are all classified as members of the autoimmune disorder group (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)). These conditions share a common underlying mechanism of immune system dysregulation that leads to tissue‑specific damage, whether in the bone marrow, red blood cells, joints, or coronary vessels. Recognizing their placement within the autoimmune spectrum aids clinicians in identifying shared pathogenic pathways, selecting appropriate immunomodulatory therapies, and monitoring for related autoimmune comorbidities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1482
retrieved_docs.content: The classification “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses several distinct conditions that share an autoimmune basis. Within this group, التهاب المفاصل is recognized as an autoimmune form of arthritis, فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد represents cold‑agglutinin autoimmune hemolytic anemia, and التهاب الشريان التاجي denotes inflammation of the coronary arteries driven by autoimmune mechanisms. Although فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية is listed among these entities, it is in fact a hemolytic anemia that does not arise from autoimmunity and therefore should be distinguished from the true autoimmune members of the “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” category. Recognizing which disorders are truly autoimmune is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic targeting, and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1239
retrieved_docs.content: The collection “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” comprises a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions that share an autoimmune basis. Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is considered an autoimmune disorder, as are autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية) and cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد). Arthritis (التهاب المفاصل) and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are also members of this autoimmune group, illustrating the systemic nature of immune‑mediated disease. Although non‑immune hemolytic anemia (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية) is listed among these entities, it does not result from autoantibody‑mediated red‑cell destruction; its inclusion reflects clinical overlap and underscores the importance of distinguishing immune from non‑immune mechanisms when evaluating hemolysis. Recognizing that these diverse conditions are linked by an underlying autoimmune process helps clinicians diagnose, monitor, and manage patients with immune‑driven pathology more effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1385
retrieved_docs.content: The SMQ category of immune disorders/autoimmune comprises a diverse group of hematologic and rheumatologic conditions. Within this category, aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is recognized as an autoimmune-mediated failure of bone‑marrow production. Non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) is also classified as an autoimmune disorder in which the marrow does not adequately replace red blood cells. Acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب) and its cold‑agglutinin subtype (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد) are both autoimmune hemolytic processes in which antibodies target red blood cells, leading to their premature destruction. In addition, inflammatory arthritis (التهاب المفاصل) is included as an autoimmune joint disease characterized by chronic inflammation and tissue damage. Together, these entities illustrate how autoimmune mechanisms can affect both blood formation and joint health, underscoring the importance of recognizing their shared immunologic basis for accurate diagnosis and coordinated patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1539
retrieved_docs.content: Wiskott‑Aldrich anemia, aplastic anemia, arthritis, and cold‑type hemolytic anemia are all classified as members of the immune disorders/autoimmune (SMQ) category. By grouping these diverse conditions together, the classification highlights that each involves dysregulation of the immune system, whether through auto‑reactive mechanisms, immune‑mediated destruction of blood cells, or inflammatory joint disease. Recognizing these diseases as part of the broader autoimmune spectrum aids clinicians in understanding their shared pathophysiology, guiding diagnostic evaluation and informing therapeutic strategies that target immune pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1392
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, arthritis, acquired hemolytic anemia, cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified as members of the autoimmune disorder group (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية SMQ). This categorization reflects that each of these diseases involves dysregulated immune activity that targets the body’s own tissues—whether the bone marrow in aplastic anemia, synovial joints in arthritis, red blood cells in the two forms of hemolytic anemia, or the coronary arterial wall in coronary artery inflammation. Recognizing these conditions as part of a common autoimmune spectrum highlights shared pathogenic pathways and can guide clinicians toward integrated diagnostic approaches and therapeutic strategies that address underlying immune dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 919
retrieved_docs.content: Arthritis, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, cold‑type hemolytic anemia, non‑immune hemolytic anemia, adrenal insufficiency, and coronary arteritis are all classified within the MedDRA Standardized MedDRA Query for immune disorders/autoimmune conditions (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية SMQ). This grouping reflects that the majority of these disorders—arthritis, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, cold‑type hemolytic anemia, adrenal insufficiency, and coronary arteritis—are driven by dysregulated immune mechanisms, such as autoantibody production or inflammatory immune cell activity. Non‑immune hemolytic anemia, although not caused by an autoimmune process, is included in the same SMQ to ensure comprehensive pharmacovigilance and safety monitoring for any hemolytic events that may arise in the context of immune‑modulating therapies. Recognizing these conditions as members of the autoimmune SMQ aids clinicians and researchers in identifying patterns of immune‑related pathology, assessing drug safety signals, and guiding patient management strategies that address underlying immune dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2276
retrieved_docs.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية) encompasses several distinct conditions, including التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). Each of these diseases is characterized by an inappropriate immune response that targets the body’s own tissues, leading to joint inflammation, destruction of red blood cells, or inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities share a common autoimmune basis aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and applying immunomodulatory therapies across this spectrum of immune‑mediated illnesses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 66
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified within the “Other deficiency anemias” group, which also encompasses folate‑deficiency anemia, iron‑deficiency anemia, thalassemia, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, cold‑type (cold agglutinin) hemolytic anemia, non‑immune hemolytic anemia, hereditary hemolytic anemia syndrome, and acquired hemolytic anemia; the group serves as a parent concept for acquired hemolytic anemia. Thalassemia is a member of the immune disorders/autoimmune SMQ, linking it to other autoimmune conditions such as arthritis, acquired hemolytic anemia, and coronary artery inflammation, and it shares a clinical association with sickle‑cell‑related retinopathy, indicating overlapping vascular complications. Autoimmune hemolytic anemia is associated with low serum calcium levels, reflecting a metabolic complication of hemolysis. The hereditary hemolytic anemia syndrome belongs to the chronic kidney disease SMQ and is incorporated in Pediatric Nephrology Terminology; it is related to crescentic glomerulonephritis, a renal pathology that is also a member of both the chronic kidney disease SMQ and the immune disorders/autoimmune SMQ, underscoring its autoimmune component. Cold agglutinin disease and non‑immune hemolytic anemia are included in the immune disorders/autoimmune SMQ despite differing pathogenic mechanisms, illustrating the broader clinical and coding context that groups diverse hemolytic and vascular inflammatory disorders together. Collectively, these hierarchical classifications, membership links, and clinical associations provide an integrated framework for understanding the pathophysiology of anemia subtypes, immune‑mediated disorders, and renal diseases, and they guide comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1332
retrieved_docs.content: Arthritis, acquired hemolytic anemia, autoimmune‑mediated hemolytic anemia, non‑immune hemolytic anemia, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions (SMQ). This grouping highlights that arthritis can arise from autoimmune mechanisms, while hemolytic anemias may be either driven by autoantibodies (autoimmune hemolytic anemia) or occur through other, non‑immune pathways (non‑immune hemolytic anemia). Coronary artery inflammation is likewise recognized as an autoimmune manifestation affecting the vascular system. Understanding that these diverse diseases share a common autoimmune framework aids clinicians in recognizing overlapping pathogenic processes and in applying immunomodulatory strategies where appropriate.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 713
retrieved_docs.content: The category “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions, including فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (aplastic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (hemolytic anemia not caused by autoimmunity), التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). These entities are grouped together because they involve immune system dysregulation, whether through autoantibody‑mediated attacks, cell‑mediated immune injury, or broader immune‑related inflammatory processes. While most of the listed disorders are driven by autoimmune mechanisms, the non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia is included in the classification for organizational purposes despite its different pathogenesis. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying immune component helps clinicians identify common diagnostic pathways and consider immunomodulatory therapies across different organ systems, thereby improving patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2327
retrieved_docs.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)) includes a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions. Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is classified as an autoimmune disease, as is non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) and acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب). Inflammatory joint disease (التهاب المفاصل) and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are also considered members of this autoimmune group. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying autoimmune mechanism guides clinicians toward appropriate immunomodulatory treatments and vigilant monitoring for systemic involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 123
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified within the broader group of other deficiency anemias, which also comprises folate‑deficiency anemia, iron‑deficiency anemia, thalassemia, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, cold‑type (cold agglutinin) hemolytic anemia, non‑immune hemolytic anemia, and hereditary hemolytic anemia syndrome. Acquired hemolytic anemia is a child concept of other deficiency anemias. Thalassemia, besides being a type of other deficiency anemia, is clinically associated with sickle‑cell retinopathy and is listed as a member of the autoimmune/immune disorders Standardized MedDRA Query (SMQ). Acquired hemolytic anemia, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, other hemolytic anemias, and cold‑type hemolytic anemia are also members of the autoimmune SMQ, reflecting their immunologic basis. The hereditary hemolytic anemia syndrome is incorporated into pediatric nephrology terminology and is a member of the chronic kidney disease SMQ, linking hemolytic disorders to renal pathology. Crescentic glomerulonephritis, also part of pediatric nephrology terminology, belongs simultaneously to the chronic kidney disease SMQ and the autoimmune disorders SMQ, illustrating overlap between renal inflammation and autoimmunity; arthritis is similarly included in the autoimmune SMQ. In Arabic, cold agglutinin autoimmune hemolytic anemia, hemolytic anemia not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified as members of the autoimmune disorders SMQ, underscoring that each condition involves immune mechanisms—whether through autoimmunity, potential immune‑mediated pathways, or inflammatory responses. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that deficiency and hemolytic anemias, autoimmune disorders, renal diseases such as chronic kidney disease and crescentic glomerulonephritis, and vascular inflammation are interconnected, highlighting the importance of integrated assessment of hematologic, immunologic, and nephrologic conditions in patient care.
